---
title: "Project Ankara Water Reservoir Analysis"
---

Welcome to my/our project page.

Keep an eye on this space to stay updated with my project activities.


# 1. Project Overview and Scope

Water management is not just an environmental concern; it is a critical optimization problem for any metropolitan city. Ankara, with its growing population and fluctuating precipitation patterns, requires a data-driven approach to ensure long-term water security.

The scope of this project is to analyze the historical occupancy rates of Ankara’s dam system. By transforming raw data into actionable insights, we aim to move beyond simple observation and provide a structured look at the city's water availability trends from 2015 to the present. The ultimate goal is to identify patterns that can assist in future urban planning and resource allocation.

# 2. Data

## 2.1 Data Source

The primary data for this study is retrieved from the ASKİ (Ankara Water and Sewerage Administration) Transparency Portal. The portal provides daily updated metrics regarding the city's water supply, ensuring the data is both authentic and authoritative.

Access Link: ASKİ Transparency Portal

## 2.2 General Information About Data

![Fotoğraf Alt Yazısı](assets/clustring_.png)


The dataset contains daily records of Ankara’s reservoirs. Key features include:
Date: The specific day of measurement.
Total Capacity: The maximum volume the reservoirs can hold ($m^3$).
Occupancy Rate (%): The percentage of the total capacity currently filled.
Active Volume: The usable portion of water stored in the dams ($m^3$).,
Daily Consumption: The amount of water distributed to the city on that specific day.

## 2.3 Reason of Choice

I chose this dataset for several strategic reasons:

Relevance: As an Industrial Engineer based in Ankara, resource optimization—especially for a vital resource like water—is a core area of interest.

Complexity: The data exhibits strong seasonal patterns and potential long-term trends, making it an excellent candidate for advanced statistical analysis in R.

Public Impact: Identifying potential water shortages before they occur is a proactive solution to a systemic urban challenge.

## 2.4 Preprocessing

Although the raw data is provided in structured formats (Excel/CSV), several preprocessing steps are required before analysis:

Data Integration: Combining monthly reports into a single time-series dataframe.

Type Conversion: Converting date strings into Date objects and ensuring occupancy rates are treated as numeric variables.

Cleaning: Handling any missing values or anomalies in the daily reporting using dplyr and !is.na() logic.

Feature Engineering: Creating "Season" and "Month" columns to facilitate categorical comparison.

Final Export: Saving the cleaned environment as a .RData file for seamless loading in subsequent analysis steps.

# 3. Analysis



## 3.1 Exploratory Data Analysis

In this section, we will visualize the distribution of water levels and use histograms to identify the variance in occupancy rates. We will also utilize line plots to compare year-over-year performance to see how 2026 compares to previous decades.

#| label: eda-annual-totals
#| code-fold: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

annual_summary <- clean_data %>%
  filter(Year < 2026) %>%
  group_by(Year) %>%
  summarise(Total_Inflow = sum(Inflow, na.rm = TRUE))

ggplot(annual_summary, aes(x = factor(Year), y = Total_Inflow, fill = Total_Inflow)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#AED6F1", high = "#1B4F72") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Total Annual Water Inflow to Ankara Dams (2020-2025)",
       x = "Year", y = "Total Inflow (m3)") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma)


## 3.2 Trend Analysis

We will analyze the "long-term" movement of water levels to see if there is a statistically significant decrease in average occupancy over the last 10 years, independent of seasonal rains.

#| label: seasonal-trend-plot
#| code-fold: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false

ggplot(clean_data, aes(x = Month, y = Inflow, group = Year, color = factor(Year))) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Monthly Seasonal Analysis (2020-2026)",
       subtitle = "Seasonal fluctuations in dam inflow",
       x = "Months", y = "Inflow (m3)", color = "Year") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

## 3.3 Model Fitting

Using R’s decomposition tools, we will separate the "noise" and "trends" from the "seasonal" components. This will allow us to see exactly how much of our water gain is purely seasonal (spring snowmelt) vs. sustainable long-term accumulation.

## 3.4 Results

xxxxxx

# 4. Results and Key Takeaways

xxxxxx
